column1: # Principle: Whitespace insignificance — runs of spaces/tabs separate
# tokens but never produce empty tokens.
# AQL is a whitespace-delimited language at the lexical level. The
# only role of whitespace is to separate tokens; runs of any length
# are equivalent to a single delimiter. This file pins down that
# rule across the spec runner's tokenizer.
# Rules being asserted
# 1. A single space and a tab between tokens are equivalent.
# 2. Multiple spaces / tabs between tokens are equivalent to one.
# 3. Leading and trailing whitespace is ignored.
# 4. Empty input (or input that is all whitespace) produces an empty
#    result stack — NOT an error.
# 5. Whitespace inside string quotes is preserved verbatim.
# 6. Whitespace inside list literal brackets [` ... `] is just token
#    separation between elements.
# Why this matters
# A pretty-printer / formatter relies on the rule that re-spacing
# a program never changes its semantics. Pin the rule explicitly so
# any future tokenizer change (e.g. a "significant indentation"
# experiment) shows up as a regression here.
# Spec runner notes
# `tokenizeSpec` (aqleng/go/spec_test.go) handles whitespace via:
# at the start of each token. So leading whitespace is consumed
# before each token, and there is no "empty token" production —
# the loop simply moves on to the next non-whitespace.
# tokens
# (Note: TSV cells separate columns by TAB; the input column itself
# uses spaces / tabs as token separators. Row 1 below uses a row of
# extra spaces that the tokenizer should treat as single separators.)

column3: multiple spaces between tokens reduce to single

column1: 1     addq     2
addq   2   3
column3: multiple spaces between word and forward args

column1: hello world
column3: whitespace inside a string is preserved verbatim
a string of three spaces is a 3-character string
multiple spaces inside brackets reduce to single
whitespace inside a list-element string preserved

column1: # Empty / all-whitespace input.
